description:
You are acting as a capacity–planning analyst for a small private-cloud provider.
    The data-science team has dropped two comma-separated value (CSV) files in the
    directory  /home/user/capacity/data/  and is asking you to find the cheapest
    combination of servers that can host every workload.                                    

    INPUT FILES (already present):
    1. /home/user/capacity/data/workloads.csv
       Columns & meaning
         workload  – unique workload name (string, no spaces except inside the name)
         cpu       – number of virtual CPU cores required (integer)
         ram       – RAM required, in GiB (integer)
         storage   – block-storage required, in GiB (integer)
       Example of the exact on-disk content (4 data rows + 1 header row):
         workload,cpu,ram,storage
         Analytics,4,16,50
         Web,2,8,20
         Database,6,24,100
         Batch,3,12,40

    2. /home/user/capacity/data/servers.csv
       Columns & meaning
         server    – unique server id (string)
         cpu       – number of available vCPU cores (integer)
         ram       – available RAM in GiB (integer)
         storage   – available block-storage in GiB (integer)
         cost      – fixed cost for using the server (integer, same currency)
       Exact on-disk content (4 data rows + 1 header row):
         server,cpu,ram,storage,cost
         alpha,8,32,200,50
         bravo,16,64,500,90
         charlie,4,16,100,30
         delta,6,24,120,40

    BUSINESS RULES:
    • Every workload must be placed on exactly one server.
    • A server can host multiple workloads, but the **sum** of the cpu/ram/storage
      values of all workloads assigned to that server must never exceed that
      server’s capacity in any of the three dimensions.
    • The optimization objective is to minimise the **total cost of the distinct
      servers that are actually used** (sum of their “cost” column values).  
      There is no per-workload cost.

    WHAT YOU MUST DELIVER
    After solving the problem with any open-source LP/MILP solver that is available
    inside the container (for example the “pulp” Python package that ships with the
    CBC solver), create the following artefacts:

    1. Directory structure (create it if it does not exist):
         /home/user/capacity/output/
         /home/user/capacity/logs/

    2. Log file  /home/user/capacity/logs/run.log  
       • Plain text.  
       • Must contain **exactly two lines** (plus the trailing newline at the end  
         of each line):  
           Line 1 – the UTC timestamp when the optimisation started, formatted  
                     as ISO-8601 (YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssZ)  
           Line 2 – the UTC timestamp when the optimisation ended  +  the phrase  
                     “ – SUCCESS” (including the leading space, the dash and the  
                     word SUCCESS).  
                     Example: 2025-03-21T10:15:42Z – SUCCESS

       A test will verify that the second line ends with the literal string
       “ – SUCCESS”.

    3. Result file  /home/user/capacity/output/plan.json  
       • Strictly valid UTF-8 JSON, no comments, no trailing commas.  
       • The file MUST match the *exact* key layout shown below; otherwise tests
         will fail.  Numeric values must be encoded as numbers, not strings.

         {
           "workload_allocation": [
             {"workload": "<name>", "assigned_server": "<server>"},
             …
           ],
           "selected_servers": [
             {
               "server": "<server>",
               "total_cpu_used": <int>,
               "total_ram_used": <int>,
               "total_storage_used": <int>,
               "capacity_cpu": <int>,
               "capacity_ram": <int>,
               "capacity_storage": <int>,
               "cost": <int>
             }
             …
           ],
           "objective": <int>,
           "feasible": true
         }

       Additional keys, different key names or different ordering will cause an
       automated check to fail.  The rows inside the two arrays may appear in any
       order, but the set of data they contain must be correct and complete.

    COMMAND-COUNT GUIDANCE  
    A well-prepared engineer can complete everything in roughly 5–10 shell
    commands (install solver, write/run a small Python script, make directories,
    move or copy the output file, etc.).  There is **no** hard limit imposed by
    the grader; this is solely a practical hint.

    BEGIN!
    (Use the terminal to inspect the CSVs, write your optimisation script,
    run it, and produce the two artefacts exactly as defined above.)
truth:
<!--
    PRE-EXISTING FILES
  -->
  <preexisting>
    <file path="/home/user/capacity/data/workloads.csv"><![CDATA[
workload,cpu,ram,storage
Analytics,4,16,50
Web,2,8,20
Database,6,24,100
Batch,3,12,40
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/capacity/data/servers.csv"><![CDATA[
server,cpu,ram,storage,cost
alpha,8,32,200,50
bravo,16,64,500,90
charlie,4,16,100,30
delta,6,24,120,40
]]></file>
  </preexisting>

  <!--
    EXPECTED OUTPUT FILES
  -->
  <expected>
    <file path="/home/user/capacity/output/plan.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "workload_allocation": [
    {"workload": "Analytics", "assigned_server": "bravo"},
    {"workload": "Web", "assigned_server": "bravo"},
    {"workload": "Database", "assigned_server": "bravo"},
    {"workload": "Batch", "assigned_server": "bravo"}
  ],
  "selected_servers": [
    {
      "server": "bravo",
      "total_cpu_used": 15,
      "total_ram_used": 60,
      "total_storage_used": 210,
      "capacity_cpu": 16,
      "capacity_ram": 64,
      "capacity_storage": 500,
      "cost": 90
    }
  ],
  "objective": 90,
  "feasible": true
}
]]></file>
  </expected>
